steps.title: Read the histogram to establish the data
steps.content:
The histogram shows 14 salmon weights distributed as:
  • 70 to under 75: 2 salmon
  • 75 to under 80: 1 salmon
  • 80 to under 85: 2 salmon
  • 85 to under 90: 6 salmon
  • 90 to under 95: 3 salmon
The new weight of 32 lbs is below every original value, so it becomes the new minimum.

steps.title: Track the median positions
steps.content: Original median with $14$ values: average the $7$th and $8$th values. After adding $32$, the new data set has $15$ values, so its median is the $8$th value. Since $32$ is inserted before every original weight, the new $8$th value is the original $7$th value.

steps.title: Use cumulative positions
steps.content:
Locate the original $7$th and $8$th values from the histogram:
  • Positions $1$-$2$: 70 to under 75
  • Position $3$: 75 to under 80
  • Positions $4$-$5$: 80 to under 85
  • Positions $6$-$11$: 85 to under 90
Both original median values are in the 85 to under 90 bin. They could be equal, so the new median could equal the old median. The median is not guaranteed to decrease.

steps.title: Test whether the mean must decrease
steps.content: Every original salmon weight is at least $70$ pounds, so the original mean is also at least $70$. The added weight is $32$ pounds, which is below the original mean. Adding a value below the mean pulls the mean down, so the mean must decrease.

steps.title: Evaluate all choices
steps.content:
• A (I only): The median need not decrease — the 7th and 8th values could be equal, keeping the median the same. Eliminated.
  • B (II only): The mean must decrease because every original weight is above 32, so adding 32 pulls the mean down. B is correct.
  • C (I and II): Since the median is not guaranteed to decrease, both statements cannot be guaranteed. Eliminated.
  • D (Neither I nor II): The mean must decrease, so “neither” is false. Eliminated.
